title: Should reinforcement learning run university course timetabling?
subtitle: A five-lens deliberation — practitioner, academic, skeptic, economist, historian — with every claim traced to a source and every disagreement kept on the record, not smoothed over.

status.headline: Not independently verified.
status.detail: This brief was generated without live retrieval (deterministic demo). Sources are illustrative and one (S-010) is explicitly synthetic. An independent review flagged 2 low-credibility sources and left 3 contradictions open for a human. Verify every source before relying on anything here.

bottom_line: On current evidence, reinforcement learning should not replace CP-SAT/MIP for university course timetabling. Exact and metaheuristic methods reliably satisfy hard constraints and produce auditable schedules, whereas RL's demonstrated strengths in combinatorial optimization come mostly from routing/packing studies and rarely beat strong OR baselines on constrained scheduling. RL's plausible near-term role is narrow: augmenting soft or dynamic objectives (e.g. disruption rescheduling), and only where a validated simulator/reward and a reproducible baseline exist — neither of which the literature currently provides for this domain. The decision is therefore inconclusive in RL's favour: keep solver-based timetabling, optionally pilot learning-augmented OR, and commission the missing benchmark before any RL investment.

strongest_findings.text: University course timetabling is NP-hard but routinely solved to feasibility/optimality by CP-SAT/MIP and metaheuristics.
Published RL-for-combinatorial-optimization gains concentrate on routing/packing and seldom outperform strong OR baselines on constrained scheduling.

supported_by.note: Mazyavkina et al. 2021 survey; Bengio et al. 2021

challenged_by.note: vendor benchmarks claim higher gains (low-credibility source S-010; unresolved X-001)

strongest_findings.text: Historically, each new 'autonomous scheduling' wave over-promised and settled into niche or hybrid use.
RL solutions lack the optimality guarantees and explainability that student-facing decisions often require.

challenged_by.note: explainability may be addressable with hybrid architectures (not yet validated)

lens_snapshot.title: Lens posture snapshot
lens_snapshot.summary: Skeptic, academic, and historian lenses lean strongly against replacing CP-SAT/MIP; practitioner keeps only a narrow pilot path open; economist requires measured operational value.
lens_snapshot.scale_label: 0 low posture intensity · 1 high posture intensity

lenses.name: practitioner
lenses.note: RL may help dynamic rescheduling only if CP-SAT remains the system of record.

lenses.name: academic
lenses.note: The literature does not establish RL superiority for constrained course timetabling.

lenses.name: skeptic
lenses.note: Hard constraints, reproducibility, and explainability remain unresolved.

lenses.name: economist
lenses.note: ML budget is hard to justify without measured rescheduling labor or disruption frequency.

lenses.name: historian
lenses.note: Prior autonomous scheduling waves settled into bounded or hybrid use.

contradictions.stake: Are reported RL gains reproducible enough to expect complementary value? (skeptic vs practitioner)
'Use RL' = replace the solver, or augment soft/dynamic objectives?
Current evidence (insufficient) vs near-term pilot potential
ML budget opportunity cost vs operational-resilience value
No peer-reviewed RL-vs-CP-SAT head-to-head for this domain

options.name: A · Keep CP-SAT/MIP as primary; no RL
points: Proven feasibility/optimality, auditable, lowest total cost of ownership.
Limited adaptivity to soft or dynamic objectives.
options.when: most universities with stable, constraint-heavy schedules.

options.name: B · CP-SAT/MIP primary + pilot learning-augmented OR
points: Keeps optimality guarantees; evidence-backed direction; incremental and reversible.
Engineering effort; uncertain marginal gains; needs ML capacity.
options.when: large institutions with ML capacity seeking efficiency gains.

options.name: C · Add an RL layer for dynamic rescheduling only (guarded pilot)
points: Requires a validated simulator/reward; governance & explainability risk; no domain benchmark exists.
options.when: only as a pre-registered pilot with a CP-SAT baseline and human oversight.

options.name: D · Replace the solver with end-to-end RL
points: No optimality guarantee; no domain evidence; high operational and trust risk.

next_actions.text: Commission or locate an ITC2007-based RL-vs-CP-SAT benchmark before any RL investment.
Quantify per-incident rescheduling labour and disruption frequency by institution type.
Prototype learning-augmented OR (learned branching / warm-starts) feeding CP-SAT (Option B).
If exploring RL, first build and validate a disruption simulator and reward, with explicit governance for student-facing automation.
Keep CP-SAT/MIP as the system of record throughout.

gaps.text: No peer-reviewed head-to-head of RL vs CP-SAT/MIP on university course timetabling.
No validated simulator/reward for institution-specific disruption dynamics.
No data on per-incident rescheduling labour or disruption frequency.
Open question: can learned components reliably warm-start CP-SAT at university scale, and what reward avoids proxy-objective drift?

sources.title: Google OR-Tools — CP-SAT Solver (official docs)
sources.note: Authoritative for solver capabilities; not a comparative benchmark.

sources.title: Metaheuristic-based Techniques for University Course Timetabling (Lewis, 2008)
ML for Combinatorial Optimization: a Tour d'Horizon (Bengio, Lodi & Prouvost, 2021)
RL for Combinatorial Optimization: A Survey (Mazyavkina et al., 2021)
Bridging Theory and Practice in University Timetabling (McCollum, 2007)
Neural Combinatorial Optimization with RL (Bello et al., 2016)
sources.note: Foundational but not domain-specific; lower evidentiary weight here.

sources.title: Attention, Learn to Solve Routing Problems! (Kool et al., 2019)
Synthetic vendor blog (illustrative)
sources.note: Low-credibility anecdote included to exercise the review stage — not a real citation.

minor: Open contradictions remain for human review: X-001, X-004, X-005
